title: Large Three-Sided Outdoor Bar
summary: A 7ft U-shaped serving bar framed in 2x4 with cedar-picket siding, a 2x12 countertop, and a notched inside shelf.
description:
This is a serving station, not a table — the U-shaped layout puts a bartender inside the three walls with guests standing around the outside, and the notched inside shelf keeps bottles and mixers within reach without cluttering the countertop.

The frame skips pocket-hole joinery entirely and relies on straightforward screwed butt joints, which makes it a faster build than most bar or kitchen-island plans at the cost of a little more visible fastener work on the inside faces — acceptable here since the siding covers every outward-facing surface anyway.

Lay the countertop boards **bark-side up**. On flatsawn lumber the bark side cups upward as it dries, which sheds rainwater instead of collecting it in a shallow bowl on an outdoor surface.

tools.note: Or a miter saw for the framing crosscuts.
Joins the countertop boards before they're set onto the frame.
Notches the inside shelf to fit around the frame.

cutList.note: Cut to fit as the side frames come together.
Notched with a jigsaw to fit around the frame.

steps.title: Cut the framing, siding and countertop stock
steps.body: From the 2x4 cut two front top/bottom rails at 72", eight vertical studs at 35-1/2", two front horizontal pieces at 21-3/4", one front middle horizontal at 22-1/2", four side wall top/bottom rails at 31-1/2", two side wall center horizontals at 28-1/2", two side top trim at 41-1/4" and one front top trim at 73". Check the board count before you shop: those twenty-two pieces take ten 8 ft 2x4 rather than the nine listed, because the eight 35-1/2" studs claim four boards two to a board and only the short blocking fits in what is left over. From the 2x12, cut the 83-1/2" front countertop plus one 32-1/2" side countertop off the first 10 ft board, and the 72" inside shelf plus the second 32-1/2" side countertop off the other. Cut six side trim at 35" from the three 6 ft 1x3, two per board. The fourteen cedar pickets divide neatly with no waste: seven go on the front at their full 72" with no cut at all, and the other seven each cut in half to give the fourteen 35-1/2" side siding boards.

steps.title: Build the front wall frame
steps.body: Lay the two 72" rails parallel on the ground and set four of the 35-1/2" studs between them, one at each end and two spaced inside, which makes the front wall 72" wide and 38-1/2" tall overall. The plan's own numbers confirm that layout: 72" less four 1-1/2" stud thicknesses leaves 66" of open bay, and the three blocking pieces are 21-3/4", 22-1/2" and 21-3/4", which is exactly 66". Screw through the rails into the end grain of each stud with two 2-1/2" exterior screws per joint, then fit the three horizontals across the three bays at a common height. Check both diagonals before you side it — this frame is the reference that the two side walls, the trim and the countertop all register to.

steps.title: Side the front wall
steps.body: Nail the seven 72" cedar pickets across the front frame with 1-1/4" brads, starting flush at the bottom rail and working up. At 5-1/2" wide, seven boards cover exactly 38-1/2" — the frame's full height — so butt each board tight to the one below rather than spacing them, or you will run short of coverage at the top. Put at least two nails per board into every stud and rail behind it; brads driven into siding alone will let the cedar cup as it dries outdoors.

steps.title: Build and side the two side walls
steps.body: Build each side wall the same way from two 31-1/2" rails and two of the remaining 35-1/2" studs, one stud at each end, with the 28-1/2" center horizontal fitted between them — 31-1/2" less two 3-1/2" studs is 28-1/2", so that piece proves the layout is right before you screw it. Stand each side wall against an end of the front wall to form the U, screwing through the side wall's end stud into the front wall's end stud with 2-1/2" exterior screws at the top, middle and bottom. Then side each side wall with seven of the 35-1/2" picket halves, running the courses the same direction as the front so they line up around the corner. The side siding is cut to fit as the walls come together, so hold each board in place and mark it rather than trusting the 35-1/2" figure, and rip the last board's width if needed to land flush at the corner.

steps.title: Fit the trim
steps.body: Cut and attach the side and front trim pieces along the top edge of the siding, fitting each one to the assembled walls rather than trusting the cut list numbers exactly.

steps.title: Notch and install the inside shelf
steps.body: Notch the 2x12 inside shelf with a jigsaw so it wraps around the inside corners of the frame, then screw it in place.

steps.title: Build and attach the countertop
steps.body: Join the three countertop boards with pocket screws, orienting each board bark-side up so it sheds water instead of cupping into a bowl. Attach the completed top to the bar frame.

steps.title: Finish
steps.body: Finish the entire bar the same way you'd finish an exterior deck or fence.
